text: Laparoscopic anterior resection for rectal neuroendocrine tumor. This fifty six year old gentleman was diagnosed to have a rectal tumor on CT scan and colonoscopy. It was found to be a tumor in the upper rectum. The biopsy revealed grade one neuroendocrine tumor. You can see the CT scan and the DOTA scan shows the high uptake in the rectal tumor with nodal metastasis in the mesorectum as shown here.

The surgeon begins laparoscopic dissection by mobilizing the redundant sigmoid colon from its attachment to the pelvic floor. The left ureter is clearly visible during the procedure. Using Ensil as an energy source, the upper rectal area is identified and mobilized, with careful preservation of nerves in the pre-sacral area. Dissection progresses in the pelvis on both the right and left sides, with lateral ligaments being dissected and divided using monopolar and bipolar diathermy, as well as Ensil. Small nodes in the mesorectum are removed with the specimen, and the dissection continues deep into the pelvis in the pre-sacral area.

The surgeon dissects the area of the pedicle, carefully isolating the inferior mesenteric artery using a hook and right angle instrument. Hemolog clips are applied to the artery before it is divided. The vein is similarly dissected, clipped, and divided. The resection entry area is nearly complete, with care taken to avoid damaging the seminal vesicles. A monopolar hook is used for resection, followed by the use of endo staplers to transect the colon. A circular stapler is employed for the anastomosis after the colon resection.
The resected specimen is displayed following the completion of the low anterior resection procedure.
